speaker: interviewer
text: --Weidman. Mr. Weidman, we-- you told us about how you could see Berlin being bombed. And you said the end of the war was near. Would you tell us what happened, how your stay in Ravensbruck ended and what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toward the end of April of 1945, either the 28th or 29th of April-- I don't remember the exact date-- they started evacuating our camp going west. They had no, no facilities to transport us, so we had to march. And we were marched. During that march-- during that march, a lot of people who couldn't march were killed. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to interrupt you for a second, would you have an estimate of how many people approximately marched with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The entire camp, it was a few thousand people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Few thousand people.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few thousand people. And we were marching west. I will make it briefer now. A few days later, we were liberated. And the Soviet Army came. And finally we found ourselves free people.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to backtrack for a second. You were marching. How many days were marching?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were marching a few days. I couldn't tell you exactly how many. But I would say maybe four or five days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you fare during that march. How did you-- how did you take the march? How did-- how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as a matter of fact, I escaped during the march with a group of my Polish friends. Because we knew this is the end-- then end of it. If we continued with this march, they'll kill us at the end of the war. And we escaped. We escaped by neutralizing two SS men. We were bivouacked in the woods for the night. And we-- we-- I gave out the orders to my group, what to do. To be sure-- they were older SS, by the way, guarding us. The youngest, they were on the front line already. And it was very easily to overcome them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I broke up our group-- we are six of us-- three and three. We neutralized two SS, tied them up and took away their weapon. And we went back to that village during the day and saw some Polish people working in a farmhouse in the village. And we went that night.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knocked on the door. These Polish people came out, and we asked them for-- for bicycles. They gave us-- they gave us six bicycles with some food along, and civilian-- civilian suits. Everybody got a civilian-- we put on top of our striped uniforms, civilian suits. But the striped uniforms were supposed to be our only identification. Otherwise nobody knew who we are. And we went west. And in the morning, there was a big panic among the Germans. And they were-- they were fleeing-- fleeing west in panic, by horse and wagon, by cars, by bicycles. If you mingled in with that group and-- and we continued west till, till, till the-- till we were liberated. We were liberated. And that was about, I would say about May 7 or May 8, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know we're sort of short of time, but I would like you to tell a little bit in detail how you were liberated, how you remember it.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was such an ecstasy to be free people all of a sudden that you could not imagine it. We, we, we didn't know what to do with ourselves. We didn't know what to start with, how to start with life again. But we went to a-- we went to a German farm, and German farmers were very much afraid of ex-prisoners because from concentration camp. They thought that they'll be killed by all of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we forced them to give us two horses with a platform. We made a-- we made a Polish flag, red and white from two rags. And we-- and we turned around, and were riding back to Poland. And we came-- to take a few days. On the way we had  some revelry. We were staying-- we had-- we had a good time those days-- I won't go into detail-- till we came to the Polish border. The Soviets took away the horses from us. They confiscated them. And we went-- we went across. Let me interrupt you just for-- for one second. You were liberated by the Russians. You got the horses and the platform. Who were-- who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five Polish friends, those five Polish friends, they were always with me. They were-- we created like a little family. We call it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these your fellow Masons from--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were not. Some of them-- some of them-- a couple of them were Masons, and some of them helpers, laborers. They work in our group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were all these people who you had met in Ravensbruck? Or you picked them up on the-- on the way? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they-- in Ravensbruck. They were all from-- prisoners-- ex-prisoners from Ravensbruck, all of my friends. I shared my package with him. My package I shared with them. And they-- they were my loyal friends. And they looked up to me. I was like their leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now you were on your way to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came to Poland, and we  were there-- we were there a relatively short time, less than a year. I saw-- I met-- I met with my father and with my sister. They came from-- from Ukraine, from Drohobycz. They came with a transport of ex-Polish citizens. They came-- I met them in Cracow. This was the first time I saw that they are alive. There was-- that-- the meeting was really-- was really very emotional.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We met. I didn't-- didn't recognize my sister. I left her she was a little girl, and here she was a grown woman. She put on weight too by then. I didn't recognize-- I recognized a friend of hers. I did recognize her at the railroad station, because I knew they were coming the transport. Somebody told me that the transport come from Drohobycz that day. And I was at Cracow at the time. And I met them. And we lived in Poland-- we lived in Poland till '46, maybe till July '46. But they-- but there were pogroms in Poland. Those prisoners that were coming back liberated, to-- back to their hometowns, the Polish people were killing them because they were afraid they will take away their-- their-- their former possessions. And they would kill them. There were pogroms. The pogrom in Kielce-- there was a known pogrom in Kielce in 1946. This was the signal for us to leave Poland. This is not a place to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: In order for me to understand exactly, how did your father and your sister manage to escape the actions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father-- my father and my sister, after my mother was killed-- she was killed right in Brunnlitz and near Drohobycz. The mass grave is where she was killed. As a matter of fact, she managed to write a little memo from-- probably hours before she was killed-- in her dress-- to my father and my sister, that they should save themselves. Do anything possible to save themselves. As a matter of fact I have that. Those, those-- this memento I have from my mother, which was found in her dress. Which, of course, they brought the dresses from the killing fields. They brought them back to town. And my father was looking through those dresses, wanted to find my mother's dress. Sure enough, he found it with that note. And I have the note. And they went-- then they went to the woods, to the mountains, with a group of sort of partisans. And they survived there in the mountains till the-- till the Soviet army came in 1944 and liberated it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. All right, now we will continue please from-- you were in Cracow. You met your father and your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- then-- then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In July '46, after the pogrom Kielce, we decided to escape from Poland. Poland is a place for Jewish people anymore. It's a bloody, bloody land. And it's not for us. And if you left-- we smuggled the border to Czechoslovakia to Vienna. We came to Vienna. And we were in a DP camp in Vienna. And I-- as a matter of fact, I matriculated on the Technical University of Vienna. I studied there. And from there, from Vienna, we went-- about '48, we went to Paris. And I matriculated on the Sorbonne in Paris. I studied there, in Paris. And then I went back to Germany. My father and my sister emigrated to America. They got a visa. I didn't. I couldn't get one. I went back to Germany. And from Germany-- in Germany I got a visa. And in 1950, July 1950, I emigrated to the United States. My father-- but then my father, as it turned out, three months of his arrival to the United States, he suffered a stroke from all these-- all these suffering that he had during the war. He developed extremely high blood pressure. He suffered a stroke. And for nine long years, I had to support my father-in-law in a nursing home. I had to-- when I-- when I came to America, I had to grab anything possible to earn some money. I went to work in factory, a garbage factory-- and to support and to support myself and to support my father. But while I was in Germany, I met my future wife. And in 1951, I went back to Germany. They sent me-- they sent me a-- a-- a card to board a ship, the I went with the back to Europe, and I went back to Germany. And I brought my wife with my in-laws in 1951, in the fall, they came on the We came to America. And in 1952 we got married. The-- the beginning of my life in America was pretty hard. As I said, I worked in a factory. And the earnings were minimal. I just barely made it. But in time, with perseverance, I made-- I made some, a few-- a few smart moves, some smart investments. I made land investments. And it-- the value grew substantially. And then I became a builder. And I moved to-- we lived in Manhattan. From Manhattan I moved to-- to Spring Valley, where I live now. And I built quite a bit of developments in this area. And I became quite-- quite financially successful. My wife gave me two sons. And one of my sons presently is a-- is a CPA, a big tax expert. He works in the Wall Street area. And he has four children. He got married. He lives-- he lives not-- around the corner from me, practically. My youngest son is a physician. He lives in Jersey. He's very-- very successful practice. And he has two-- two-- he gave me two grandchildren, a son and a daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now I'm semi-retired. And-- And what happened to your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister-- my sister moved to Omaha, Nebraska. And she lives there. She still lives there. She's a widow at the present time. She had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father passed away in 1958. He passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. It's a fascinating story. And I wonder if there's anything at this point what you would like to say maybe as a concluding word

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to your adventures and your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to be honest with you, for years I couldn't talk about my experiences. I was like at first in a shell. I couldn't talk about it. But as I grew older, and I realized they cannot take-- I have to share my experiences with future generations. They should know what happened, how the Jewish people suffered, what they went through, how they were destroyed, to be a warning for the future generation to prevent it from happening. And I feel that my story maybe is not different from others. But I had to share it and tell the story. It was not easy for me to tell it. But I hope no generation will have to retell such a story again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mr. Weidman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is a picture of myself when I was five months old. This picture was taken in Schodnica and mailed to my aunt in America. On the other side of the picture, there is a dedication from my-- in my father's handwriting, dated the 28th of October 1923. I am five months old. And to a-- for a souvenir, for my-- for my-- about my dear son, signed my father and Clara, who's my mother. This group picture was taken in Schodnica by the front entrance to our house. It's to the-- the first row, to the right is myself. I was six years old. Next to him is my grandfather. Then is my grandmother. In the left is my sister. She was two years old at the time. The rear row, to the left is my mother, and to the right is my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture represents my Latin class in Blatt's Gymnasium. In the very-- in the back in the rear is Professor Blatt. I myself, I'm on the, on the left side in the first row. From the whole class is alive only one fellow. He lives in Paris-- in the last row on the left hand side. And the third row to the left side, is another friend of mine, lives in Montreal. And in the right side, they were all destroyed except for, in the last row on the left side, is a friend of mine. She lives in France. And also, she died last year. She was a professor of psychology at the Hebrew University. She died in Jerusalem. On the left side, in the row, the one before the last on the-- on the right hand side. And it was Blanka Burg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Weidman, would you tell us what year the picture was taken and in what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Drohobycz in the Gymnasium Blatta, Blatt's Gymnasium, as I said. It was take approximately 1937 or '38. This picture is an enlarged picture from my so-called kenncarte, and just before the ghetto was opened up. As you can see, the right-hand corner, you can see part of the German eagle. And it says-- and it says in German, [GERMAN]. That was identification paper. There was a short while, a few months-- it was taken in 1942. There would be two or three months before I escaped from Germany-- to Germany. This is the last picture from those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken, Mr. Weidman?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Drohobycz-- in Drohobycz before the ghetto was opened up. This is the original piece of paper that my mother wrote just before she was executed with hundreds of other people in Brunnlitz, Poland. And that little piece of paper says in Polish, to my father and my sister-- they were still there. They should save themselves by all means. And that piece of paper was saved-- it was found in her dress that was brought brought back from the place of execution. And I preserved this piece of paper in this-- in this plastic covers for a-- as a souvenir for my future-- for the future generations to see. This is a picture of the pocket of my striped suit that I wore in the concentration camp Ravensbruck, with my number on it. Beneath that number was a red triangle with a P on it, designated me as a Polish political prisoner. I-- I saved this pocket, so the only-- my only souvenir that I have from those times of being a prisoner in concentration camp Ravensbruck. And this is my number. This is a pin saying Ravensbruck, my number, and my triangle. This is the pin. It was made in 1945 after liberation. There was a style, all the ex-prisoners were wearing it proudly in their lapels to show off that they were ex-prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Weidman, what does the P stand for on there?

speaker: interviewee
text: P stands for Pole. There was a-- I was a Polish man. And the red triangle stands as  a political. I was a political prisoner, Polish political prisoner. This pictures is of myself. It was taken approximately in 1946 or '47, a couple of years after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where was taken? It was taken probably in Vienna. This picture was taken at my youngest son Joshua's wedding in 1992. And the front on the right side, that's myself. And then there's my first granddaughter Rachel, and my wife. In the rear row is my oldest-- right-hand side-- is my oldest son Steven. Then there is bride and groom, Dr. Joshua Weidman and his bride Cherie. To the left is my daughter-in-law Paula with a second grandchild, Rebecca.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Weidman, now that you've concluded the interview, it's time to introduce your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: To my right is my son Joshua Weidman with his lovely wife next, Cherie, and their daughter Allie. And Joshua is holding his-- my youngest grandson-- grandchild rather-- Ethan. To my left is my wife Dina. And to her left is my daughter-in-law Paula. She's the wife of my oldest son Stephen, who couldn't participate today because he is working. And on the bottom row, to the right is my grandson Shlomo and granddaughter-- first granddaughter, oldest granddaughter-- Rachel, and her sister Rebecca, and my youngest granddaughter, Steven's daughter. Her name is Miriam. She's a little bit restless, but she's a sweet girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would like to say about  this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like just to say, to have such a beautiful family, to live to have such a beautiful family was worthwhile to go through what I went through. And God gifted me with beautiful fruits. And I'm really enjoying it in my older age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyone else that would like to say something before the conclusion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm-- I just wanted to say that my father was a Holocaust survivor. And it's just difficult sometimes. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I just wanted to say that I admire him tremendously, and for his courage, that he was able to survive as a teenager and lose almost his whole family. All right, sweetie. And above all, that he was able to maintain his convictions, his Jewish convictions, and was able to raise a beautiful family. And if not for him, none of us seated here right now would be here today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well I thank you all very much. Anyone else who would like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anybody else?

speaker: interviewer
text: No? Well then, I guess-- yes? No? Anyone else? Or-- then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hand me the mic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Would you like to say something, Cherie?

speaker: interviewee
text: No

speaker: interviewer
text: Well then, that concludes the interview with Mr. Mark Weidman.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, no,  you change your mind?

speaker: interviewer
text: And I will thank you very much for coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's OK. He's just done.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and for participating. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
